one_line_takeaway: MedSentry introduces a 5,000-prompt stealthy medical-safety benchmark plus a topology-aware attack-defense pipeline showing Decentralized MAS resist insider 'dark-personality' agents best and SharedPool worst, with a lightweight training-free psychometric-screening + isolation defense (PCDC) that restores safety to near-baseline.
key_finding: Under single-agent dark infiltration, Decentralized is most resilient (LCS drop 2.6%, RS drop 2.7%) while SharedPool is most vulnerable (LCS drop 8.7%, RS drop 9.6%); Centralized and Layers fall in between (~3-6%). MedSentry prompts are consistently harder than MedSafetyBench (lower LCS/RS across all methods), validating its greater threat/stealth. The PCDC Enforcement Agent restores safety to near-baseline without training: SharedPool rebounds 69.1->76.0 (+7.4%, 2.4% below baseline), Centralized 72.2->74.8 (+3.5%, 1.5% below), Decentralized 80.2->81.6 (+1.8%, 0.9% below), Layers 75.0->76.1 (+1.3%, 2.6% below). Multi-round debate amplifies attacks (Centralized round-3 drop grows to LCS 17.2%/RS 18.7%) whereas Decentralized stays stable; increasing agent count improves robustness. Token-range profiling shows attack timing differs by topology (Centralized worst at tokens 700-800, SharedPool worst mid-dialogue 500-600), motivating time-aware defenses.
core_contribution: MedSentry makes three coupled contributions to the safety of medical LLM multi-agent systems (MAS). (1) A benchmark of 5,000 adversarial medical prompts spanning 25 primary threat categories and 100 subthemes, built by human-AI collaboration: three licensed physicians defined 5 domains (Medication Misuse, Dangerous Medical Advice, Medical Fraud, Vulnerable Group Risk, Scientific Misinformation), GPT-o3 proposed topics/subtopics filtered by expert review, then GPT-4o and Claude 3.7 Sonnet each generated candidate attack instructions (2,500 each = 5,000) that were physician-curated and then 'purified' to minimize explicit malicious cues (stealth). (2) The first systematic empirical comparison of four multi-agent communication topologies (Layers, SharedPool, Centralized, Decentralized) under an insider 'dark-personality' agent, formalized as M=(A,S,C,f,g) with an attack-defense evaluation pipeline. (3) A lightweight, training-free defense (PCDC / Enforcement Agent) combining a 10-item psychometric screening scale distilled from Dark Triad, PCL-R and MACH-IV inventories, behavioural verification of utterances, and topology-aware graded isolation. NOTE: MedSafetyBench, MedAgents, MetaGPT, ChatDev, Medprompt, Multi-expert Prompting are cited/baseline work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Insider threat within a collaborative medical MAS. For each run, exactly one benign agent (ak) is randomly replaced by a 'dark-personality' adversarial agent (ad), defined as any agent whose risk score R(ad) exceeds threshold tau=60. The adversary operates covertly through inter-agent messages using three tactics: (i) authority forgery (inventing citations), (ii) data traps (fabricating evidence), and (iii) consensus hijacking (feigned professional agreement), with the goal of contaminating the dialogue trace and depressing the Evaluator Agent's medical-safety score (e.g., eliciting false prescriptions, dangerous advice, or misinformation). The defender is an Enforcement Agent that screens, verifies, and isolates malicious nodes. This is a genuine security framing: attacker capability, objective, and defense are all specified.
limitations: (1) Recovery is only to near-baseline, not full; absolute gains are modest (e.g., Layers +1.3%). (2) Both the attack scoring and defense rely on LLM judges: the Evaluator Agent (AMA-based) and the psychometric screener are themselves LLMs, raising circularity and gameability concerns not stress-tested. (3) The dark-personality detection is based on self-report-style psychometric scoring with a hand-set threshold (tau=60); adaptive adversaries that evade the 10-item scale are not evaluated. (4) Only a single injected malicious agent is studied (no colluding/multiple adversaries). (5) Default setting is a single debate round with GPT-4o backbone; generalization across other backbones is limited (only GPT-4o and Claude-3.7 subsets shown, no open-weight backbone results in main tables). (6) No real clinical-workflow deployment or human patient-outcome validation (explicitly deferred to future work). (7) The AMA score range [0,90] and the LCS 100-token cutoff are heuristic design choices whose sensitivity is not analyzed.

datasets_used: MedSentry (this paper's own benchmark): 5,000 adversarial medical prompts, 25 primary topics, 100 subtopics; 2,500 generated by GPT-4o and 2,500 by Claude 3.7 Sonnet
MedSafetyBench (baseline comparison dataset): 1,800 harmful prompts (900 GPT-4, 900 jailbroken Llama-2-7b), used as adversarial inputs
quantitative_results: Attack degradation (mean drop): Decentralized LCS 2.6% / RS 2.7% (most resilient); SharedPool LCS 8.7% / RS 9.6% (most vulnerable); Centralized ~ LCS 6.0% / RS 3.6%; Layers LCS 3.2% / RS 4.5%

PCDC defense recovery: Centralized 72.2->74.8 (+3.5%, 1.5% below baseline); Decentralized 80.2->81.6 (+1.8%, 0.9% below); Layers 75.0->76.1 (+1.3%, 2.6% below); SharedPool 69.1->76.0 (+7.4%, 2.4% below)

Table 2 baseline safety (MedSentry GPT-4o subset, LCS/RS): Decentralized 83.4/83.2 (highest), ChatDev-like 80.2/78.4, Layers 80.1/78.2, SharedPool 76.4/77.9, Centralized 77.2/76.3; all MedSentry scores below MedSafetyBench counterparts (e.g. Decentralized GPT-4 subset 83.7/83.6)
Multi-round amplification: Centralized attack drop grows from LCS 6.0%/RS 3.6% (round 1) to LCS 17.2%/RS 18.7% (round 3); defense gain rises to +17.2%
Agent-count ablation: SharedPool attack drop shrinks from 13.2%(LCS)/13.4%(RS) at 3 agents to 8.8%/8.4% at 6 agents; Decentralized stays 1-3% throughout
Token-range worst-case LCS deltas: Centralized -3.0 at tokens 700-800; SharedPool -2.0 at 500-600; Decentralized -1.3 at 200-300; Layers -0.8 at 900-1000
Evaluator score range S in [0,90]; dark-personality risk threshold tau=60; default 5 agents, temperature 0.7, single debate round, GPT-4o backbone
baselines_compared: The four MedSentry core topologies: Centralized, Decentralized, Layers, SharedPool
MedSafetyBench (as a competing benchmark for RQ1 validity)
